sentences.text: It was a-- a rainy, cold, foggy, gloomy, gray, terrible day.
Very appropriate for the arrival.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They took us through those gates, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and-- and an entirely new world opened up for me, as-- as for an adult, or as for a child, or for anybody seeing emaciated people in their-- in their garbs with the stripes, and hats, and pushing wagons, and carrying tools, and-- [SIGHS] and barbed wires everywhere, and everywhere.
The-- The lager was sectioned to different areas, and the guard towers around it, and all over, barking dogs, the German Shepherds.
Here and there, you see people beating inmates there.
[SIGHS] If you wanted to ask me what my feelings were, I-- I could not describe it.
That was the end, and really, I felt this was the end.
So they took us to an area which was separated.
We were a few thousand people there.
Barracks, with bunk beds, three bunk beds on-- on top of each other.
And that's where we found our accommodation.
To describe the life in Bergen-Belsen, there was a certain system.
There was a certain monotony of-- of life there.
You had to get up very early in the morning and go outside, all the thousands of us, and stand.
The Germans would call it zahlappell.
They would count us.
They were counting us.
All the time, counting us.
I could never understand, why did they count us so much.
If we were so valuable or-- or they just bookkeeping?
The proper German bookkeeping.
The food was terrible.
It was some kind of soup made of of some kind of bulbs that was to feed cows.
And parts of horses sometimes were seen in that soup.
The bread was made half of sawdust.
It was brown, and brick shaped, and brick taste, and hard as a brick.
But even that-- as the days were passing, even that was more than treasure.
You-- You needed to eat.
Our family was together.
Thank to the list, we were still together.
And actually, on one of the barracks, there was an inscription that we were exchanged to Switzerland.
So there was hope for us that maybe that's going to happen to us.
That's why our family was kept together.
And so we were thinking, why are we different from anybody else on the outside of this perimeter of the camp?
The difference was that the families were kept together.
That's-- And that was life or death, when your family was together.
Even this little meager food, my mother would always give me a part of her-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] her bread, and her soup, and whatever.
And also, she said we have to keep clean.
If we don't keep clean, we will die.
So there were people that they didn't shave, they didn't comb, they didn't wash.
One day, he looked disheveled, the next day he looked dirty, the third day.
And a week later, the man had already fleck typhus and died.
That was the first time I encountered corpses.
That was the first time, as a matter of fact.
And as a physician, that was the first time that I have to say my encounter with a dead human body, with a corpse.
I never saw a dead man before.
I-- I was isolated and never went to a funeral.
That's what's a very remote, mysterious thing which I really never even wanted to think of before as a child.
14 and a 1/2.
Well, when somebody died--
--and that happened very often now, in a very short time.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When somebody died, they put the corpses next to the barrack, and they put them like firewood, in a row, very orderly fashion, as the Germans do.
As the frost came-- already in November, December, it was very, very, very cold-- you might know that Bergen-Belsen is in the north.
It's between Hanover and Hamburg in Germany.
It's cold country.
So these corpses would-- would freeze, and-- and they [? would put ?] them.
[SIGHS] And when I saw it first time, it-- I just [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] absolutely couldn't understand, why is it happening, and what is-- what is death, really?
What-- This is death?
And eventually, you know, week after week, you see this, you don't even pay attention anymore.
Not even as a child.
Another dead body, another dead person.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] People were losing weight rapidly.
People were sick.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] As I can see, our second biggest enemy, outside of the Germans, were-- were the lice.
We had lice, lice in our hair, the hair lice.
We had lice in our clothing and lice in the pubic hair.
We had lice everywhere.
And later on, this-- these little bugs turned-- turned to be our-- our worst enemies.
Because the majority of the people died afterwards of fleck typhus.
Typhus from-- from the lice.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There was a very tall man from Hungary, tall, way over 6', like somewhere, 6' 4', 6' 5', who also wear a-- a fur hat.
And since he was so tall, the Germans-- the SS point at him, you're the biggest man here.
You're going to be the lager commander.
So he-- he commanded us, and he-- he was a lagerfuhrer.
He was not a very pleasant person, a Jewish man.
And--
Fischer.
That was Mr.
Fischer, who, we learned later, would steal food, and steal bread, and give it to his cronies, or try to-- to trade it for some valuable, if somebody still happened to have a ring or something.
He was prosecuted after the war, when he survived and came back to Hungary.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I would stand at the barbed wire, and look outside and see what's happening in some other areas of-- of the lager.
And I saw people going out to work, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] bent shoulders and emaciated people, with those terrible bland looks and-- and grimness.
And as I can remember, the weather was always bad.
It's-- It was always cold, or raining, or foggy.
I don't remember-- I don't remember the sun.
Maybe I don't remember it well, maybe it's my mood, but the sun-- the sun is totally out of my picture of remembrance.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One day, I looked outside and I saw a woman in a uniform with a stitch, blonde hair, with an old man coming across with the striped clothing and hat.
And she stopped him.
And apparently, he didn't take off his hat in front of her, or something happened.
I-- was farther away from me.
And she hit the-- the hat off his head, and then he beat the man.
He-- She beat that man, she.
And he collapsed, and then came a-- a big wagon with large tires, rubber tires, which was full of corpses.
It was like a mountain of corpse.
And two Jewish inmates came, picked up this old man-- [? we ?] was just beaten to death in front of me.
They threw him up on the-- on the wagon, and-- and tooked him.
I saw the--
I was told afterwards-- I didn't know-- but I was told it was Ilsa Koch.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] We did not go to work.
We didn't go anywhere.
All we did is just counting in the morning, Zahlappell and hanging around, and waiting, waiting--
--for our destiny.
We don't know why, but many, many people already died, because we ate the same food.
And so I learned later that Bergen-Belsen was really a starvation camp.
The Germans had experiments also, on minimizing calories, and how much a person can survive on.
What was the minimum calorie, and it was obviously-- was not enough for us there.
We had a doctor in our barrack.
The Jewish doctors were distributed in-- in different barracks, but they practically had nothing to treat us with.
You had aspirin, I think.
Well, there wouldn't-- no effort from the Germans.
I could not understand it.

Now, I cannot understand it, because the Germans were so afraid of diseases, that I-- I can't remember any-- any effort or-- or any-- any cleaning, even, facilities to-- to get rid of your clothing or changing.
Nothing.
I can't remember.
In one of these-- Again, I-- I encounter somebody-- it-- it-- somehow, my good angels who-- who looked after us always provided with some good people along the line.
We-- We were having these zahlappell in the morning, and there was an old German sergeant, a-- an SS sergeant, had big, bushy eyebrows and the Nazi hat.
And he had a pipe, always a pipe, so we called him Popeye.
And this German came, and he came very close, and I was shaking, you know, when you're stand there in the line.
And came next to me, the little boy, and he put a piece of bread in my pocket.
And then next day, again, we were in the barrack-- in the barrack, and he would come in and we jumped up.
And he said, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
And I'd remembered his face, this-- this old, wrinkled face, with the big, bushy eyebrows and the pipe, and Popeye.
And he did this several times.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Let me finish this story by jumping forward, after maybe a year or two after my liberation, being in Hungary, I picked up a-- a magazine.
I don't remember, unfortunately, what the name was.
[SIGHS] Oh, unfortunately, I don't have the magazine.
And I see a picture of a uniformed German man, with the bushy eyebrows and a pipe.
I said, this is Popeye.
It said, and this German SS sergeant worked for the British intelligence.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So there are some providence somewhere that keeps helping you along.
Well, what-- what--
All I could see that this-- this wagons with the corpses, and-- and the beatings, and the-- and the people.
And one day, some planes were flying over the camp, and leaflets were falling down.
And we had severe instructions not to pick up any leaflets under death penalty.
Nevertheless, somebody picked it up, and we went to the barrack and-- and read it.
And it was that the Allies broke through in the-- in the Western front.
And [?
Aachen, ?] which was a German city was taken by the Allies.
So there was a glimmer of hope if somebody could survive this, then maybe-- maybe one day, we'll be saved.
The first ones who died, really, were the children.
Interestingly, medically-speaking, I found that people who were in their 40s or 50s, they survived more.
The young ones died first.
Young people, say, in their 20s or-- or younger children could not take the starvation.
Their metabolism is different, and they were the first to die.
All my children, all the children who were with me in Austria of my age, they all died.
I had a little second cousin in Hungary and his name was Steven Torok, T-O-R-O-K, with an umlaut.
Steven had curly hair, and a big smile on his face, and he was a bit retarded.
Not very much, but just a little bit, but he always liked to play with us.
So we would say, Steven, let's go to play.
First, we eat, then we play.
And that was what Steven always said.
First, we eat, then we play.
Steven loved to eat.
And he was the first to die in Bergen-Belsen.
He just couldn't take it.
I don't know why I-- I was privileged to live.
Maybe to tell my story.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Slowly, slowly, I-- I became ill, and I lost terrible-- I had a weight loss, a terrible weight loss, began to cough all the time.
I felt feverish and things didn't look too good.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] One day, the Germans came and they told us, get outside.
You line up, all of you.
I don't know how many of us [INAUDIBLE].
I couldn't tell you, was it 1,500 and 2,000, how many people, or even more.
They lined us up and they said, we're gonna the camp.
That was after a bad winter.
And it was cold and-- and snowing.
By that time, it was April.
Spring didn't bring any beauty to this place, I must tell you.
I still don't remember the sunshine.
All I remember that the snow was melting, and everything was dirty and filthy, and-- and the conditions were not good.
They were worse.
There were more corpses, and more people died.
And eventually, they stood us up and they said, we're going to leave.
So we marched out from Bergen-Belsen.
We marched again to the railroad station and they placed us in a boxcars in a long train.
And we were not told anything.
We didn't know anything about the destination.
But as we learned later, we were taken south-- southeast from Bergen-Belsen.
The journey began on the train again.
And this train and the-- and the-- and the-- the rattling of the rails, it's still in my ears.
It-- it somehow, these train-- the journeys in the trains and the cattle cars made-- made-- made a terrible impression on me.
Because every time we were in them, they always took us to something worse.
Every time the train left, I knew that it's something worse will come, and maybe ends in death.
So they took us.
One day, we were going.
The train was going and stopping, faster and slower.
The SS was with us everywhere.
The SS were sitting in regular passenger cars, and we were in cattle cars.
And I got sicker and sicker.
I was-- I had fever.
Now, if I would say, I probably had 104 temperature, with shaking, chills, and coughing.
And I begin to have pains in my chest and both sides, stabbing pains.
Every time I would breathe, I felt stabbing on both sides and I could hardly breathe.
I was short of breath.
I was weak.
I couldn't practically stand up.
I was lying down in the-- in the boxcar, my parents, my sister around me.
And nobody knows what, why, and there was no-- no help, no cure.
Another day passed, and it was going on.
The train was moving again in a slow pace and faster pace.
And maybe the third or fourth day [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] before that I celebrated my birthday.
The 10th of April, 1945, I became 15.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS] To me, the celebration was to-- to get a deep breath and get enough oxygen.
So on-- Another day passed, and another day passed, and the train, each time, was going slower and slower.
And we began to hear some unusual noises like woo-woo, distant cannon or distant thunders.
What-- I really didn't know what they were.
But they were battlefield noises, probably cannons, or tank cannons, or bombings.
We didn't know what it was.
But these noises became stronger and stronger, and were getting closer to us.
So on the day of April the 13th, the plane stopped--
Not the plane, the train.
The train stopped and the engine decoupled.
The engineers took off with the engine.
They left the train, without an engine, standing there, the middle of the-- the rails in a forest.
Actually, next to a forest.
And we're standing there and standing.
And somebody looked outside through the barbed wired window and saw that SS was tearing off their insignia.
They were tearing off the skull and bones, and the SS, and some of them were changing-- changing to civilian uniforms.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was nothing.
There was silence.
There was absolutely nothing, and that went on hours and hours.
And I could really hardly breathe.
And suddenly, you heard some rambling, like a-- like a machinery rambling in the forest.
Have you ever heard a tank moving?
Well, that was the noise.
And somebody hollered in the train, the Americans are here!

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, when I read somewhere that in a Japanese prison camp, were-- there were terrible conditions, as we all know, there were the American and British prisoners who, for many, many months, or a year, maybe, I don't know how long, and suddenly, a Japanese comes in and he says to them, war over.
You go home.
What I want to tell you is the meaning of a word, the meaning of that sentence to those people, or the meaning of this word to me, the Americans are here!
It's indescribable.
It is-- It is the difference between life and death, it-- between slavery, prison, and liberty, between a-- a future and a black past.
It's-- It's life.
It's sunshine.
So we looked outside, and we see the tanks rumbling, rambling, and they coming out from the forest.
And soldiers with-- with strange uniforms, which I've never seen, ever, strange helmets.
All I was accustomed to see, the SS, the gray SS uniform.
And here come these people in green uniforms, and-- and the guns, and they come and surround the train.
And they opened the doors and people fall out, and-- around there.
And then they hugged these people.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Where this is the acme, this is the highest point of-- of my life.
[SIGHS]
OK.
At [? least-- ?]
